name: Hina
description: Hina is a prominent figure in Polynesian mythology, appearing in various forms and holding diverse roles across different island cultures. She is often depicted as a goddess of the moon, femininity, childbirth, and the arts, particularly tapa making and weaving. Her origins are shrouded in the mists of time, with stories varying from island to island. In some accounts, she is a mortal woman who ascends to godhood, while in others, she is a primordial being. Hina possesses powers related to her domains, such as controlling lunar cycles, influencing tides, and bestowing fertility. She is revered for her wisdom, beauty, and skill. Her relationship with other deities, such as Maui, is central to many narratives. She is sometimes depicted as his wife, mother, or sister, and their interactions often involve Hina seeking Maui's aid to overcome challenges or obtain resources. Hina's role varies across Polynesia; she may be a powerful goddess, a skilled craftswoman, or a nurturing mother figure. Regardless of her specific manifestation, she represents the feminine principle and the cyclical nature of life.
subtitle: Goddess of the Moon, Femininity, and Crafts

keyMyths.title: Hina and the Moon
keyMyths.description: Hina, tired of the earthly world, seeks refuge on the moon, becoming its guardian. She brings light and regulates the tides from her celestial home.

keyMyths.title: Hina and the Coconut Tree
keyMyths.description: Hina sends her son to bury her head, which grows into the first coconut tree. This act provides sustenance and resources for humanity.

keyMyths.title: Hina and Maui's Quest
keyMyths.description: Hina needs a rope to secure her canoe, so she sends Maui on a quest to obtain it from a giant eel, demonstrating her resourcefulness.
